You might be aware that there are mathematical structures called "matrices" as well, studied in linear algebra and the like. It's just a coincidence that the two ideas use the same word; logical matrices don't have anything to do with those matrices, as far as I know.

The three example matrices a couple of paragraphs back weren't chosen at random: respectively, they determine classical logic, K3, and LP, once you know how to look at them. Here's the idea: one way to present a logic, the way we've been looking at, is to specify some kind of valuation, and then say what it takes for a particular valuation to be a counterexample to an argument. The logic presented in this way counts an argument as being invalid if any valuation is a counterexample to it, and valid if no valuation is a counterexample.

Now, suppose you have a matrix, which is to say you have a structure together with some set \Delta of the structure's values. (That's the Greek letter Delta. Remember, I'm using Greek letters for sets; I've chosen \Delta because "designated" starts with "d".) The valuations you get from this matrix are just whatever valuations you'd get from the structure. The set \Delta isn't involved at all in determining these valuations. The set \Delta is instead involved in saying when one of these valuations is a counterexample to an argument. A valuation v is a counterexample to an argument \Gamma \;/\; A exactly when v takes every premise in \Gamma to some value in \Delta , but takes A to a value outside of \Delta . We use this to get, for every matrix, a particular logic that the matrix determines.

fr:title.text: Counterexample in a matrix, logic determined by a matrix

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Counterexample in a matrix, logic determined by a matrix

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
Given a matrix with structure S and set \Delta of designated values, a valuation v in the structure S is a counterexample to an argument \Gamma \;/\; A in that matrix iff both:
for every B in \Gamma , v(B) is in \Delta , and
v(A) is not in \Delta
The logic determined by a matrix is the logic that contains an argument iff that argument has no counterexamples in the matrix.
All our earlier examples fit this pattern; this is the key idea for this week.

The set \Delta of designated values here is playing the role of the values we're counting as "true". So in K3, where we don't usually think of \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} as being true, we don't have \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} as a designated value. But in LP, where we do usually think of \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} as being true, there we do have \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} as a designated value. This gives us an example where we can have two different logics, coming from two different matrices, built on the same structure. The only difference is in the set of designated values.

fr:title.text: Another example logic, presented with a matrix

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Another example logic, presented with a matrix

fr:mainmatter.html:p: To give you some practice with this idea, here's another example logic. This one I'll present directly via a matrix. The structure we'll use for this one is the Dunn-Belnap structure, and the designated values are \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, \mathsf {\mathbf {B}}. There; that's enough to fully specify a logic. This one is one we'll call FDE.

"FDE" stands for "first-degree E". E is a logic like FDE, but E uses a very different implication connective. The logic E was first developed to use this implication connective to give some understanding of entailment; that's why it's called E.

E is a member of a family of logics known as relevant logics (in Australia) or relevance logics (in the US). Relevant logics form a very important aspect of Australian research in logic---indeed, they're the reason that I first came to Australia. They're a bit too complex for these notes to spend much time on them, but if you want to look into them, you'll find a fascinating literature.
Like all the logics we've seen so far, FDE holds the argument p \land q \;/\; p to be valid, and p \lor q \;/\; p to be invalid.
Take a moment and see if you can find an FDE counterexample to this second argument.

There are a number of different ways to reach an FDE counterexample to the second argument. If you have a valuation v where v(p) is either \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} or \mathsf {\mathbf {N}} and where v(q) is either \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} or \mathsf {\mathbf {B}}, then you have a counterexample to this argument; if your valuation isn't like this, then you don't have one.

Let's think it through: to be a counterexample to p \lor q \;/\; p, a valuation would need to take p \lor q to one of our designated values \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, \mathsf {\mathbf {B}}, and take p to some undesignated value. Since the undesignated values in this matrix are \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, \mathsf {\mathbf {N}}, we know that for v to be a counterexample we need v(p) to be either \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} or \mathsf {\mathbf {N}}. What, then, about v(q)? Well, we also need v(p \lor q) to be either \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} or \mathsf {\mathbf {B}}. By checking how the Dunn-Belnap structure treats disjunction, you can see that, with v(p) being either \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} or \mathsf {\mathbf {N}}, setting v(q) to either \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} or \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} will work, and nothing else will.

What about the arguments that distinguished K3 and LP from classical logic? Here, too, FDE is different from everything we've seen so far. Like K3, and unlike classical logic or LP, FDE holds \;/\; p \lor \neg p to be invalid; any valuation v where v(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {N}} provides a counterexample. And like LP, but unlike classical logic or K3, FDE holds p, \neg p \;/\; q to be invalid; any valuation v where v(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} and v(q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} or \mathsf {\mathbf {N}} provides a counterexample. For the same reason, FDE agrees with LP in holding disjunctive syllogism to be invalid, unlike classical logic or K3.

By now, you have a lot of different ideas to keep track of, and it's important to keep them clearly distinguished from each other. On one track, you know about languages, and then arguments, and then logics: a language is made up of atomic formulas and connectives; and then an argument is a set of formulas together with another formula; and then a logic is a set of arguments. On a separate track, you know about structures, valuations, and matrices: a structure is some values together with a specification of how the connectives work on those values; a valuation is any way of assigning values from a structure to the atomic formulas, which then results in assignments of values to every formula; and a matrix is a structure together with a set of designated values.

The key notion that ties these two tracks together is the notion of a counterexample, as you've seen. Given a matrix, a particular valuation is a counterexample to a particular argument when that valuation assigns designated values to all the argument's premises and also assigns an undesignated value to the argument's conclusion.

Then, with all that in hand, you can see how, given any particular matrix, we can determine a logic: given a matrix, we count an argument being invalid in the matrix's logic when some valuation is a counterexample to the argument, and valid otherwise.

Every one of these terms---structure, matrix, valuation, language, argument, logic, counterexample---means something different from all the others. It's a lot to keep track of, I know. Just try to choose your words carefully, and know that I'm doing the same.
We've also looked at some of the features these logics have in common, and at some of the features that distinguish them.

html:ul.html:li:
Section A: Preliminaries
Lecture 1: Introduction and languages
Lecture 2: Functions and substitutions
Lecture 3: Sets, arguments, and logics

Section B: Structures and matrices
Lecture 4: Structures
Lecture 5: Matrices and counterexamples
Lecture 6: Using matrices to compare logics
Lecture 7: The limits of matrices

Section C: Exploring intuitionistic logic
Lecture 8: Natural deduction
Lecture 9: Intuitionistic logic
Lecture 10: Intuitionistic models
Lecture 11: Harmony

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Welcome to symbolic logic class! I bet I'm going to have a fun semester in here, and I hope you do too.

To start out, you might expect me to tell you what logic is. But I've been a logician for a while now, and I don't know the answer to that, so that's not how I'm going to start out. I can tell you, though, that logic is abstract and requires close attention to detail; that will help you start to get the vibe.

We're going to spend the semester looking at some things that logicians do. These things might be hard to see at first. They're pretty mathsy, but there are no numbers. They have something to do with the languages we speak, but only indirectly. A ton of logic is done by philosophers, but it's then put to use in places like electrical engineering and computer science, where philosophy isn't typically applied. One thing is for sure: there's a lot of weird symbols. (Hence symbolic logic.)

The reason we're going to use lots of weird symbols is the same reason logic turns out to be useful in lots of different endeavours: because of how abstract it is. It turns out, for example, that views held by some philosophers thinking deeply about the nature of truth map exactly on to microscopic structures in the flash storage in your phone. This semester, we won't be thinking too deeply (other than recreationally) about either the nature of truth or about how flash storage works---but we will see and study closely what they have in common.

Throughout these lectures, I'll include a number of side notes like this one, marked "Side note". These are not essential to understanding the core of the unit, but they might be of interest. (Of course, they might not!)

The word "logic" gets used in a lot of different ways. Sometimes, it's associated with a certain style of debate, involving DESTROYING an opponent with FACTS and LOGIC. That's got nothing to do with what logicians actually do. Our work this semester is constructive, not destructive; doesn't involve opponents at all; and centers delicate and precise reasoning, not cheap dunks.

Working at an abstract level means that what we do can at the same time be shallow and widely applicable. By lifting ourselves out of the details of individual topics, we lose a lot of important sensitivity to the way those topics work.

At the same time, it helps us think thoughts that turn out to be useful in surprisingly diverse ways, and to find patterns in one area that recur in another area, even when those areas don't seem related. Most of the time, then, we're going to be operating at too abstract a level to be guided by ordinary intuitions. And without the details of individual applications to keep us honest, we won't be able to say things like "oh, you know what I mean".

That's why close attention to detail becomes so important; it's all we'll really have to hang on to. You do develop logical intuitions over time, through practice and exposure. But that can take years, and has to kind of happen on its own. In this class, then, instead of intuition, we'll be focusing on doing things explicitly, carefully, and deliberately.

The kinds of activities we'll be looking at and doing this semester have a history: they really kicked off in the mid to late 19th century in Europe and the US, among some philosophers and mathematicians. Between then and now, these activities have turned out to be central to one of the most important developments of the 20th century: computers. Logic is directly involved in both the hardware and the software of all computers; it was instrumental in their invention, has been a constant companion in their development, and today guides the development of a wide range of computer systems. As computers have come to take a central place in our societies, then, so too has logic.

Logic is certainly not limited to applications involving computers, though. (For example, it's still a central part of philosophy, where it is rarely connected to computers at all.) Nowadays, you can find logic all over the world, and you can find logicians working in philosophy, mathematics, linguistics, computer science, electrical engineering, artificial intelligence, cognitive science, psychology, and elsewhere. Logic also tends to be done in relatively identifiable geographic styles: there's identifiable Dutch logic and Brazilian logic and Australian logic, among others.

I'm originally from the US, and I was educated there. But I had the good fortune to encounter the Australian tradition early on in my logical life (thanks to a professor who had spent years working in Australia). It's this Australian logical tradition that brought me to Australia in the first place.

fr:title.text: Enough sales pitch already; what are we going to do?

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Enough sales pitch already; what are we going to do?

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Mainly we're going to focus on a few different ways to think about information, meaning, content, that kind of thing. When we think about meaning, it's often useful to distinguish the thing that carries meaning from the meaning itself. Lots of different things carry meaning: pictures, sounds, sentences, books, social media posts, this very lecture, and so on. We're going to work with vehicles for meaning that are abstract and symbolic. These vehicles will be introduced presently; they make up what we'll call our languages.

The languages give us vehicles for meaning, but they don't on their own have any meaning; they're just empty carriers. Over the course of the semester, we'll look at two different ways logicians study for giving meaning to these languages. One way involves thinking about connecting the languages to something outside of themselves, things like truth values, propositions, possible worlds, or states of an electric wire. We'll call this way model-theoretic. The other way involves thinking about interconnections within a language, rather than connections to something else. We'll call this way proof-theoretic. The two approaches are complementary, and once we have them both on the table, we'll move back and forth between them and look at some important ways they're connected.

There are a few logicians' habits you should know about, to be able to make the most of these lectures and our conversations. These are habits of expression that are completely standard among logicians, but can throw you off if you're not used to them. I'm flagging these habits here so that you can be ready. I'll try to remember to flag them as well where I use them, but these are so ingrained in me by now that it's entirely likely I won't notice some.

If you notice some that I haven't flagged, let me know! That way, I can correct the lectures. The same goes for any typos or errors of any sort, or recommendations in general. These lectures get revised every time the unit runs; they've improved a lot (to their present ok-but-not-exactly-gonna-change-the-world state) as a result of past students' input. I really appreciate their input, and I'll really appreciate yours!

Whenever I say something like "BLAH or SUCH-AND-SUCH", I always mean to include the case where both BLAH and SUCH-AND-SUCH happen. I won't bother with any extra qualification like "or both", or anything like that. You can always take for granted that I mean to include the both case. So, for example, I'd be perfectly happy to say: "Melbourne is in Victoria or Mercury is the closest planet to the Sun". Both of these are true, so my "or" claim is all good.

This is the weirder one. Whenever I say something like "Every BLAH is a SUCH-AND-SUCH", I always mean to include the case where there are no BLAHs at all. Again, I won"t bother with any special qualifications; you can always take this for granted. So, for example, I"m perfectly happy to say: "All unicorns breathe fire". Since there are no unicorns, my "all" claim is all good.

For both of these, there's a case to be made that what I'm doing is out of step with what "or" or "all" mean in English (Note the use of Habit 1 there!), and there's a case to be made that it isn't. But for our purposes, it's not important to settle that question. What is important is that you see precisely what I mean when I say "or" or "all", and keeping these logicians' habits in mind is key for that.

Just about everything we're going to do (maybe actually everything!) will involve what we call languages. Our languages will be relatively simple, though; we won't look at anything as complex as a language like English. We won't even come close to that degree of complexity!

The languages we use instead have been built by logicians to be particularly suited for doing logic. That way, we can practice (and develop) logical techniques and approaches in a simple and controlled setting, without having to worry about the complications of real-world languages. (Also, it means that what we do can be applied to things that might not at first seem particuarly languagey.)

We will have a standard language that will be our main language for the semester, but we will sometimes think about other languages, too. Each language we work with will have some set of building blocks to start with, and some methods for composing larger units out of smaller ones. The languages will differ from each other in terms of what the building blocks are, and what methods for putting things together they have. Whatever we can put together from these ingredients in a given language we'll call the sentences or formulas of the language.

In some areas of logic, there is an important difference between "sentences" and "formulas"; "formula" is the more general word, with "sentences" needing to meet more restrictions. We won't worry about that at all in this unit; the distinction won't come up for any system we look at, so we can treat these words as synonyms. But do note that if you read other material on logic (or if you have in the past), these words are very much not synonyms in general!

This also shows one of the things I'll do with these side notes. Students in this class come from a very wide range of backgrounds; many of you have never dealt with logic before at all, but some of you have seen some logic before. One of my goals is to keep the class accessible to people with no logic experience, but still to allow for people who have seen some logic to draw connections between this class and what they've already seen. So there will be a fair few notes like this one about "sentences" and "formulas": off the main track and completely ignoreable, but intended to give a bit more context to people who may have encountered logic in some other situation. (If you continue your study in logic, I hope that these notes might also help you draw connections between what you're learning now and what you learn in the future!)

You can think of these languages as playing the same kind of role for logicians that model organisms like E. coli or Drosophila melanogaster (fruit flies) play for biologists: they're simple and easy to study, and have become standardised among researchers for this reason. We hope, of course, that what we learn about such simple things contains lessons for more complex things as well---but one thing at a time.

The basic building blocks of each language are called its atomic formulas. They could be anything at all; we'll use the letters p, q, r, \ldots for them. Why do we start at p? I dunno! My best guess is that it stands for "proposition", but that's really just a guess. It's very standard nowadays, but I don't know how far back it goes. (Feel free to let me know, if you know!)

"Atom" comes from ancient Greek for "not divisible", and that's how we're going to use these formulas. Each of them counts as a full formula in its own right, and as far as we're concerned there's no internal structure: no subject, no predicate, no copula, etc. Each atomic formula is totally structureless: just p, or just q, or cetera.

To specify a language, part of what we need to do is say what its atomic formulas are. One language might have just the single atomic formula p; another might have just the three atomic formulas p, q, r; another might have seventeen atomic formulas; another might have infinitely many; and another might have none at all. However many atomic formulas a language has, though, all of these atomic formulas count as formulas of the language. There can be other formulas too, but the atomic formulas of a language are its starting point for formulas.

fr:title.text: Infinity; or, more than about six

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Infinity; or, more than about six

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The last paragraph gave us our first brush with infinity. I allowed there that some languages might start from infinitely many atomic sentences. In fact, almost all of the logical languages that people actually use, and most of the ones we'll use this semester, do start from infintely many atomic sentences.

Infinite collections will accompany us throughout the semester, so it's good to get used to them. The idea of infinity can seem a bit weird at first, but that's mostly just because it's unfamiliar. We won't need to do anything fancy with infinity; we just need to be aware that it's there.

Most of the techniques we use to deal with infinite collections are the very same techniques we'd use to deal with finite collections that are very big. For example, if we think about a language with infinitely many atomic sentences, there might seem to be an immediate problem: we can't list them all! But that's not really an issue of infinity. After all, if we just had a language with, say, 10^{100} atomic sentences, that would be a finite collection, but we still couldn't list them all. That's more atomic sentences than there are particles in the observable universe, more atomic sentences than seconds since the Big Bang, etc. It's a really big number!

And since we're using lowercase letters for our atomic sentences, we start to run into trouble much lower indeed: what if we have just 27 atomic sentences? Even if we wrap around the alphabet from z to a, we run out after 26. Worst of all, I'm lazy and easily distracted, so I wouldn't ever want to have to list more than like seven or eight things at most.

There are lots of different methods we can use to handle infinities, but we're going to use the best and most standard method: not worrying about it. As it happens, when we're doing exercises or looking at examples, we'll never need to actually make use of more than five or six atomic sentences at once, and rarely even that many. More than that and it mainly just gets too unmanageable. So in these notes, I'll just always work with at most p, q, r, s, t, u, and that will be enough, for all the examples and exercises we need. Really just p, q, r are usually plenty. When a language has more atomic sentences than this, it's enough for us to just know that they're there; we don't actually need to have a separate symbol for each individual one.

So once we're working in a language with more than six or so atomic sentences, we won't ever bother listing them all. And that means that having infinitely many atomic sentences doesn't cause any special problems. We can't list them all, but we're never going to try, so no problem. It's enough to know that they're there. This method---the method of not worrying about it---will be the key method we use for handling infinite collections throughout the semester. This is probably the same method you've used to understand the infinity of counting numbers for most of your life. The kinds of infinity we'll deal with in here are pretty manageable like this.

You might wonder why we use infinite collections at all, if we're not going to think very hard about the fact that they're infinite. For our atomic sentences, there's no real reason; we could get by just fine for most of our purposes with finitely many. But in other contexts, infinity will just naturally arise on its own, so we need to be prepared.

Besides the atomic sentences, the other thing we need to specify to give a language is how more complex sentences can be put together out of simpler ones. Each way of combining sentences takes a certain fixed number of component sentences, and produces a combination of them. We'll also associate different symbols with different ways of combining sentences. These symbols, and the ways of combining they're associated with, are called connectives. To specify a language in full, we give a collection of atomic sentences and a collection of connectives.

One connective we'll use a lot this semester is \land ; we'll always use this symbol for a connective that combines two components. Because it combines two components, it's called a binary connective. For now, it's got no special meaning; it's just a symbol. (We're saying which things are sentences, not yet saying anything about what those sentences mean.) I'll pronounce this symbol "and" and call it "conjunction", which gives some foreshadowing of what we're going to use it for.

Let's consider a simple language, then, that has just the two atomic sentences p and q, and the single binary connective \land . The sentences of this language are everything we can make from these ingredients. At first, that might not seem like much: we've got p, we've got q, and we've got the sentence that uses \land to combine p with q, which we write p \land q. In fact, though, there's a lot more than this.

First, order matters for binary connectives. So in addition to p \land q, we also have the different sentence q \land p. Second, we can reuse atomic sentences; we can have as many ps and as many qs as we want. So we also have the sentences p \land p and q \land q.

But most importantly, connectives can build on any sentence in the language. So since we have the sentences p and q \land p, we also have the sentence that \land builds from these two (in that order), which we write p \land (q \land p). And since we have the sentences p \land p and p \land (q \land p), we also have the sentence that \land builds from these two (in that order), which we write (p \land p) \land (p \land (q \land p)). And so on.

You can see why it was important for us to be ready for infinite collections! Even with just two atomic sentences and just this one binary connective, this language has infinitely many sentences in it. This is because we can use \land to combine any two sentences (in either order), including sentences that we've already produced using \land itself, and so the process of building sentences can go on forever.

You probably noticed that I've used parentheses above in giving sentences like p \land (q \land p). This is really important. The connective \land is binary: it needs exactly two sentences to put together. So something like "p \land q \land p" doesn't pick out a sentence at all, since it doesn't give us two components to combine via ${\land }. The sentence p \land (q \land p), on the other hand, is fine, since it uses \land to combine the two sentences p and q \land p. Similarly, the sentence (p \land q) \land p is also fine---but it is a different sentence. It uses \land to combine the two sentences p \land q and p

To help you build sentences properly, it can help to picture a downward-branching tree kind of structure, where each binary connective branches into two, and each atomic sentence sits at the tip of a branch. For example, we could picture the sentence p \land (q \land p) like this:
And we can picture the sentence (p \land q) \land p like this:
As you can see, these are different trees, which shows that these are different sentences.

You can think of our sentences as "really" being trees in this way, if you like. Keeping this kind of tree in the back (or even the front) of your mind can be helpful as you get started with our sentences. When we're writing sentences normally, we use parentheses to indicate the same kind of structure that's encoded in these trees. There's nothing special about the parentheses; they're just a more space-efficient way to write the same thing.

Many of our languages will involve more than one binary connective. Another binary connective we'll use a lot is \lor , which I'll pronounce "or" and call "disjunction". Again, don't worry about its meaning yet; for now we're just seeing how to build up sentences using connectives.

Let's think about a slightly more complex language now, one with the two atomic sentences p, q and the two binary connectives \land , \lor . Every sentence that was in our simple language with just p, q and \land is also a sentence of this more complex language, since we have all those ingredients. But there are a lot of sentences in this new language that weren't in the old one.

First, there are all the sentences that make use of the new binary connective \lor , sentences like p \lor q, like p \lor (p \lor p), and like (q \lor q) \lor (p \lor (q \lor p)). But there are also sentences that use both binary connectives, like (p \lor q) \land q, like p \land (p \lor p), and like (q \land (p \lor p)) \lor (p \land q).

Consider the last of these: (q \land (p \lor p)) \lor (p \land q). This uses the binary connective \lor to combine the two sentences q \land (p \lor p) and p \land q, in that order. Those sentences themselves each use the connective \land to combine two component sentences. We can again picture the whole sentence as a tree, putting two branches at binary connectives and atomic sentences at the tips of branches, like so:
This tree shows the same structure that the parentheses indicate in (q \land (p \lor p)) \lor (p \land q).

A third binary connective we'll use this semester is \rightarrow , which I'll pronounce "implies" and call "implication". As a connective, it's just another binary connective, and works the same way as \land and \lor : given any two sentences, we can combine them with \rightarrow in between them to get a new sentence. All three of these binary connectives can be mixed freely, so we now have such wonderful sentences as p \rightarrow (p \lor (q \land (r \rightarrow s))) or ((p \lor q) \rightarrow (r \land q)) \lor (r \rightarrow p). And again, you can think of these as trees if you like, so that last example spells out to:
A good idea: Grab a pen and paper, and draw out the tree for the other example above: p \rightarrow (p \lor (q \land (r \rightarrow s))).

Not all connectives, however, are binary. We can have connectives that combine any number of sentences together. Some connectives might be ternary, for example, combining three sentences into one. Or some might be quaternary, combining four sentences into one. The number of sentences a connective combines is called the arity of the connective. Binary connectives, then, have an arity of 2; ternary connectives have an arity of 3; and so on.

In fact, though, we won't spend much time on connectives with arities higher than 2. In part, this is because these are just less used in lots of logic. In part, it's because there's not a super-convenient way to write them! With a binary connective, you can just put it between the things it combines, as we've done with \land and \lor . But that doesn't work as well for higher arities. (No problem putting them in trees, though; just use three branches for a ternary connective, four for a quaternary, and so on.) We will, though, spend a lot of time with connectives with lower arities: 1 and 0. These are called unary and nullary connectives, respectively.
Let me pause to lay out our first definition. Definitions are key in logic, and we'll meet a lot of them this semester.

html:ul.html:li: Every connective has an arity, which is the number of sentences the connective takes to form a sentence.
A binary connective is a connective with an arity of 2.
A unary connective is a connective with an arity of 1.
A nullary connective is a connective with an arity of 0.

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
Unary first. The unary connective we'll use a lot uses the symbol 
eg . This connective takes one component sentence to make a new sentence. I'll pronounce this "not", and call this connective "negation". Suppose we're working in a language with just the single atomic sentence p and the sole connective 
eg . Then we have all of the following as sentences: p, 
eg p, 
eg 
eg p, 
eg 
eg 
eg p, 
eg 
eg 
eg 
eg p, and so on. (Indeed, all the sentences in this language have some number (maybe 0) of negations followed by p.)

Note that we don't need any parentheses for these sentences. The sentence 
eg 
eg p applies 
eg to the sentence 
eg p; there's no other way to read it. However, when we start to combine unary and binary connectives, sometimes parentheses are still necessary. For example, consider a language with atomic sentences p, q and connectives \land , 
eg . Compare the two sentences (
eg p) \land q and 
eg (p \land q). The first of these uses \land to combine 
eg p with q; the second uses 
eg with the single sentence p \land q. Both are perfectly good sentences, but they are different, and the difference is important.

We can again see the difference using trees. Binary connectives still make two branches, and unary connectives make just a single branch. So these two sentences produce the following two trees. Make sure you pause to see which is which.

Sometimes, the number of parentheses we need can get a little bit out of control. For the most part, the answer is just practice: getting used to dealing with three or four parentheses deep is very doable, and you'll probably be totally fine even if you're just comfy with like two layers. However, there's a common convention I'll use to save on some parentheses, and you'll need to know how to read it.

The convention is this: unary connectives always attach to the smallest sentence they can. So if I write something like "
eg p \land q", it might seem ambiguous: is 
eg combining with just p, or with p \land q? Since p is smaller, though, the convention says that it combines with p. So 
eg p \land q is the very same sentence as (
eg p) \land q. If we want 
eg to combine with p \land q, then parentheses are needed: 
eg (p \land q).

When you write a sentence like (
eg p) \land q, you can include the parentheses or not; that's totally up to you, and either way is perfectly correct. But you'll need to know what I mean when I write something like 
eg p \land q: it's always (
eg p) \land q. And you'll need to include parentheses when what you mean is 
eg (p \land q); there's no other way to write this.

Finally, we have nullary connectives: connectives that take zero component sentences to make a new sentence. That is, a nullary connective is already a formula on its own. So for the purposes of building up formulas in our languages, a nullary connective is like an atomic formula. However, a nullary connective is not an atomic formula; so when we start doing things (as we will) that depend on the difference between atomic and nonatomic formulas, nullary connectives will go in with the other connectives, not with the atomic formulas.

My favourite nullary connective is \bot ; I'll pronounce it "falsum". On its own, it is a sentence; and so it works with our various other connectives just as any sentence would. For example, take a language with atomic sentences p, q, r, and connectives \land , \bot . The sentences of this language include things like \bot \land p, like (p \land \bot ) \land (\bot \land \bot ), and like p \land (\bot \land p).
Another nullary connective we'll make use of is \top ; I'll pronounce this one "verum". Since it's a nullary connective, it too is already a sentence.
A good idea: Come up with a few different sentences in a language with no atomic sentences at all, but that includes all the connectives \land , \lor , \rightarrow , \neg , \bot , \top .

So that's how all our languages will work. To specify a language, you say what atomic sentences it has, and what connectives it has. It's important to specify an arity for each connective. For our purposes, whenever you see connectives that use the symbols \land , \lor , \rightarrow , \neg , \bot , \top , these will have arities 2, 2, 2, 1, 0, 0, respectively. Any other connectives we look at will need to have their arities specified as they're introduced.

We're mainly going to stick to a particular language: one with infinitely many atomic sentences, all the connectives we've seen so far, and no other connectives. This is simple enough to be relatively manageable, while still having enough complexity in it to allow us to explore a range of ideas. Whenever another language is not explicitly specified, you can safely assume the language at issue is this one. I will call it the standard language:

Once we have a language specified, it's important to be able to categorize its sentences in a few different ways. This section will introduce some of the vocabulary we'll be using throughout the semester to do this.

We already know about atomic sentences. A compound sentence is just any sentence that isn't atomic: Ellie Ripley2026129https://negation.rocks/lectures/000U/000U/lectures/000U/Compound sentenceDefinition
A compound sentence is any sentence that is not atomic. So sentences like p \land q, or \neg r, or \bot are all compound sentences. Another way to think of it is: a compound sentence is any sentence that contains a connective.

Every compound sentence has a main connective. If you think of a sentence as being put together in stages, the main connective is the last connective used in assembling the sentence. If you write the sentence as a tree, the main connective is the one at the very top of the tree. No sentence can have more than one main connective: atomic sentences don't have any main connective at all (since they don't contain any connectives!), and every compound sentence has exactly one main connective.

So, for example, in the sentence p \land (q \lor \bot ), the main connective is conjunction, \land . At the last stage of its construction, this sentence uses \land to combine two other sentences: p and q \lor \bot . So while other connectives, \lor and \bot , do occur in the sentence, they're not its main connective. Its tree looks like this:

As you can see, this tree has the connective \land at the very top; that's another way to see that \land is the main connective of this sentence. (Note that nullary connectives like \bot go into trees in just the same way as atomic sentences: at the tips of branches.)

As another example, the main connective of \bot is \bot . This sentence is its own main connective, since that's the last connective used in building the sentence. Another one: the main connective of \neg (p \lor p) is \neg . Here's its tree:

It's often useful to categorize sentences by their main connective. So for example, we might want to think about all the sentences whose main connective is conjunction, \land . These sentences are called conjunctions; the category of sentences just takes its name from the connective name. Similarly, the sentences whose main connective is disjunction \lor are called disjunctions; the sentences whose main connective is negation \neg are called negations; and we will do the same kind of thing for every connective we come across. (There is only one sentence whose main connective is falsum: just \bot itself. The same happens for any nullary connective.) To sum this up:
The main connective of a compound sentence is the last connective used in forming the sentence. Atomic sentences have no main connective.

Sentences can be categorised by their main connectives. So a conjunction is a sentence whose main connective is conjunction; a implication is a sentence whose main connective is implication; and so on for any connective.

In general, we'll be working with sentences in different ways depending on their main connective. So it's important to be completely fluent in taking a sentence and identifying its main connective. To that end, the first exercise type you'll see at https://binchicken.one will give you practice with this: it will give you a random sentence, and your task is to identify the main connective of that sentence.

Every compound sentence has some number of components. Its components are the sentences used by the main connective in building up the compound sentence. As a result, the number of components a compound sentence has is always the same as the arity of its main connective.

So, for example, all conjunctions have two components; and the components of the conjunction p \land q are p and q. To keep track of the order they occur in, we call p its left component and q its right component. Every sentence whose main connective is binary has a left component and a right component. Let's look at some examples: the right component of (
eg q \lor p) \land  ot is  ot ; the left component of (
eg q \lor p) \land  ot is 
eg q \lor p; and the right component of the left component of (
eg q \lor p) \land  ot is p

For sentences whose main connective is unary, there is only one component. Here are some examples: the component of 
eg 
eg 
eg (p \lor q) is 
eg 
eg (p \lor q); the component of 
eg  ot is  ot ; and the left component of the component of 
eg ( ot \land q) is  ot .

A good idea:Double-check all these examples, so that you can see the idea for yourself! Remember, this is symbolic logic; there are going to be a lot of symbols. It's important to build good habits right away---don't let your eyes glaze over this stuff. Read carefully; re-read.

Sentences whose main connective is nullary have 0 components; that is, they don't have any components. The same goes for atomic sentences: no components at all. Only sentences with a main connective with arity 1 or more have components.

The components of a compound sentence are the sentences combined by its main connective. There are as many components as the arity of that main connective. Atomic sentences do not have any components.
When a sentence's main connective is binary, it has two components, which are called its left component and its right component.
Depending on the main connective of a sentence, there are special names sometimes given to its components:

html:ul.html:li: when a sentence is a conjunction, its components are called its conjuncts;
when a sentence is a disjunction, its components are called its disjuncts;
when a sentence is an implication, its left component is called its antecedent;
when a sentence is an implication, its right component is called its consequent; and
when a sentence is a negation, its component is called its negatum.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: So that gives us a start on languages. Here's a quick summary.

A language consists of some collection of atomic sentences and some collection of connectives. Each connective has an arity, a fixed number of sentences it can combine. The connectives \land , \lor , \rightarrow are all binary, which means their arity is 2; they're called conjunction, disjunction, and implication, respectively. The connective \neg is unary, which means its arity is 1; it's called negation. The connectives \bot , \top are nullary, which means their arity is 0; they're called falsum and verum, respectively. Other connectives are possible, too; these are just some standard ones we'll use.

The sentences of a language are all the things that can be built up from the atomic sentences of the language by using the connectives of the language. Our standard language has infinitely many atomic sentences and uses all the above-listed connectives.

A sentence that is not atomic is called compound. A compound sentence always contains at least one connective, and it might contain many. Every compound sentence has a unique main connective: the connective that's used last in the construction of the sentence. Sentences are categorized by their main connectives: a sentence with conjunction as its main connective, for example, is called a conjunction, and similarly for other connectives. A compound sentence has as many components as the arity of its main connective. In case the main connective is binary, these are called its left component and its right component.

The most important skill for you to take away from this is the ability to look at a sentence and identify its main connective. Since we'll be dealing with different sentences differently depending on their main connectives, you need to become fluent with this as soon as possible. The first exercise type at https://binchicken.one will help.

For now, this might all seem weird and pointless. What are these languages and their sentences for? What are they about? These are good questions, and we'll spend much of the rest of the semester seeing possible answers. Don't worry, then, if you don't feel like you have a total grasp yet. On the other hand, if you do feel like you have a total grasp already, that's almost certainly an illusion. More next time!
